segments.speaker: designer
segments.label: Designer
segments.text:
# Design Recommendations for CTO Pay Dashboard

I've reviewed the PRD, the Stitch-generated design candidate, the existing infrastructure (notably `cto/cto-web` already deployed), and the hackathon context. Here are my recommendations for the three design decision points.

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-10
category: ux-behavior
question: How should the web dashboard handle wallet connection and customer identity?
recommendation: C

OPTION_A:
label: "Full Wallet-Adapter Only"
description: Integrate @solana/wallet-adapter with Phantom, Solflare, Backpack, etc. Every interaction — even viewing receipts — requires a connected wallet. The app auto-scopes all data to the connected pubkey.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate (stitch-web-1) with a prominent "Connect Wallet" hero state replacing content until authenticated. Standard Solana dApp pattern seen on Tensor, Jupiter, Marinade.
strengths: Maximally decentralized-first per §3a. Judges immediately recognize the Web3-native UX. Wallet connection unlocks deposit/withdraw if dp-11 goes transactional. Single identity model — no ambiguity about whose data you're viewing.
tradeoffs: Requires wallet browser extension — blocks the demo if a judge doesn't have one installed. Adds ~2 days of integration work (adapter setup, connection state management, error handling for rejected connections). Overkill for receipt verification, which is fundamentally a read-only, public-data operation.

OPTION_B:
label: "Read-Only Pubkey Input"
description: No wallet connection. User pastes a Solana pubkey into a search/input field and the dashboard fetches all on-chain data for that address — balance PDA, task receipts, agent package stats. Pure read-only, works without any browser extension.
visual_reference: Similar to Solana Explorer or Solscan address lookup. The Stitch candidate layout with a pubkey search bar replacing the wallet button.
strengths: Fastest to build (~1 day). Zero dependency on browser extensions — works in any browser, any demo context. Perfectly serves the PRD's primary dashboard purpose: "customer verifies receipts" (§11.3). Judges can paste any address and explore.
tradeoffs: No write operations possible (deposit/withdraw require signing). Doesn't feel like a "real dApp" — feels like an explorer clone. Loses the decentralized-first identity principle. Anyone can view anyone's data (though this is all public on-chain data anyway).

OPTION_C:
label: "Dual Mode — Wallet Connect + Pubkey Lookup"
description: Dashboard opens in read-only mode with a pubkey search bar. A "Connect Wallet" button in the header unlocks authenticated actions (deposit, withdraw, update caps) when clicked. Disconnected users can still paste any pubkey to explore receipts and agent packages. Connected users get their data auto-loaded plus write capabilities.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate (stitch-web-1) layout with both a search bar in the nav area AND a wallet connect button in the top-right. Think of how Solscan lets you browse freely but offers wallet connection for watchlists — adapted here for transactional actions.
strengths: Best of both worlds. Demo-friendly: judges can explore without installing extensions, but the wallet-connect path is there for the live demo video. Aligns with §3a decentralized-first (wallet is the primary identity) without penalizing casual exploration. Graceful degradation — the dashboard is useful even if wallet-adapter breaks during the demo.
tradeoffs: Slightly more complex state management (two identity modes). Need clear visual distinction between "browsing someone else's data" and "viewing your own connected wallet." ~1.5 days more than Option B, ~0.5 days more than Option A (because you're building both paths).

REASONING:
The hackathon demo video (§11.3) explicitly shows "customer verifies receipts in Solana explorer" — meaning verification is a public, read-only act. But the decentralized-first principle (§3a) says wallet-based identity over OAuth. Dual mode satisfies both: the demo can show a judge exploring receipts by pubkey (no extension required) AND show the customer connecting their wallet to deposit/withdraw. This is also the most resilient demo strategy — if wallet connection fails on stage, you still have a working dashboard. The incremental cost over wallet-only is minimal.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-11
category: ux-behavior
question: Should the web dashboard support transactional operations (deposit, withdraw, register package) or be read-only verification?
recommendation: C

OPTION_A:
label: "Read-Only Verification Dashboard"
description: Dashboard displays balances, task receipts, agent package stats, and spending cap information — all fetched directly from on-chain accounts. All write operations (deposit, withdraw, register, settle) happen via the CLI. The dashboard is a viewer, not a controller.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate (stitch-web-1) with data tables and cards but no action buttons or forms. Clean, information-dense layout.
strengths: Minimal frontend scope — can be built in 1-2 days. No wallet-signing complexity. Lower risk of demo-breaking bugs (read-only operations are idempotent). Lets the CLI demo stand on its own merit while the dashboard provides visual proof.
tradeoffs: The demo video becomes split between two interfaces (CLI for actions, dashboard for verification), which is harder to narrate. Judges don't see the "full loop" in one place. Misses the opportunity to show a polished Web3 product experience. Feels incomplete — like a monitoring tool, not a product.

OPTION_B:
label: "Full Transactional Dashboard"
description: Dashboard supports all customer-facing operations: deposit USDC, withdraw USDC, update spending caps, create customer account, AND register/update agent packages (for authors). All via wallet-signed transactions built and submitted from the browser.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate with action modals/drawers for each operation. Think Marinade Finance or Raydium-style transactional UI — every card has an action button.
strengths: Most impressive demo — the entire §11.3 flow happens in one interface. Judges see a real product, not a CLI + viewer. Showcases the full Solana dApp experience. Every operation is visually narrated.
tradeoffs: Significantly increases frontend scope — transaction building, error handling for failed txns, confirmation polling, balance refresh after mutations. Adds 3-4 days of work. Agent package registration UI requires form design for split_bps, source_uri, etc. Higher risk of demo-breaking bugs (a failed transaction during the video is painful).

OPTION_C:
label: "Hybrid — Read-Only + Deposit/Withdraw Only"
description: Dashboard shows all read data (balances, receipts, agent packages) AND supports the two most demo-critical write operations: deposit and withdraw. These are simple single-input forms (amount in USDC). Agent package registration and spending cap updates remain CLI-only.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate layout with two prominent action buttons on the customer balance card: "Deposit" and "Withdraw", each opening a minimal modal with an amount input and a "Sign & Submit" button. The rest of the dashboard is read-only tables and cards.
strengths: Covers the most visually compelling part of the demo — money moving on chain via a UI. Deposit/withdraw are the simplest transactions to build (single amount parameter, predictable accounts). Keeps package registration on the CLI where it naturally fits (author tooling). Scope is ~2 days of transactional work on top of the read-only base. The demo video can show: CLI registers package → dashboard deposits USDC → CLI runs task → dashboard shows receipt → dashboard withdraws remaining balance.
tradeoffs: Package registration isn't in the UI, so the author side of the demo stays in CLI. Judges might wonder why some actions are in the UI and others aren't (though this is easily narrated). Still requires wallet-adapter integration for signing.

REASONING:
The hackathon demo (§11.3) needs to show money moving — deposit, settlement, withdrawal, receipt verification. Option A forces judges to watch a CLI, which undersells the product. Option B is the dream but adds 3-4 days of risk to a hackathon timeline. Option C hits the sweet spot: deposit and withdraw are the two operations that create the "wow" moment in the demo video (USDC moving on-chain via a clean UI), while the more complex operations (package registration, settlement) stay in the CLI where they're already built. This keeps the dashboard scope to ~3 days total while delivering the highest-impact demo moments in the browser.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-12
category: design-system
question: What component library and design system approach should the web dashboard use?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS 4"
description: Use shadcn/ui as the component foundation with Tailwind CSS 4 for styling. shadcn/ui components are copy-pasted into the project (not a dependency), giving full control. Dark mode by default to match Solana ecosystem aesthetics. Use the Stitch-generated candidate as layout reference, implemented with shadcn/ui primitives.
visual_reference: Stitch candidate (stitch-web-1) implemented using shadcn/ui's default dark theme. Data tables via shadcn/ui's table component (built on Tanstack Table). Cards, badges, and dialogs from shadcn/ui. Color tokens tuned to a dark navy/purple palette that echoes Solana's brand without copying it.
strengths: Industry-standard for new React projects in 2025-2026. Excellent dark mode support out of the box. Accessible by default (built on Radix primitives underneath). Fastest path to a polished-looking dashboard — shadcn/ui's default aesthetic is clean, professional, and very Web3-compatible. The `tweakcn` service already deployed in the cluster suggests the team is familiar with shadcn/ui customization workflows. Tailwind 4's CSS-first config and `@theme` directive simplify token management.
tradeoffs: If `cto-web` uses a different design system, this dashboard will look visually distinct from the main CTO product. shadcn/ui's aesthetic is recognizable — experienced judges may notice "that's shadcn" (though this is true of most Solana hackathon entries). Requires React/Next.js as the framework.

OPTION_B:
label: "Radix Primitives + Custom CSS"
description: Use Radix UI primitives directly (Dialog, Popover, Dropdown, etc.) with custom CSS modules or Tailwind for styling. No pre-built component library — every component is styled from scratch to match a custom design language.
visual_reference: A bespoke dark-mode dashboard with custom styling that doesn't look like any known component library. Could closely match the Stitch candidate's layout with unique visual personality.
strengths: Maximum visual distinctiveness — won't look like "another shadcn app." Full control over every pixel. Radix primitives handle accessibility and keyboard navigation so you're not starting from zero.
tradeoffs: Significantly more styling work — every component needs custom CSS instead of using shadcn/ui's pre-built variants. Estimated 2-3 extra days versus Option A for the same component coverage. For a hackathon, this time is better spent on program features. The visual distinctiveness benefit is marginal when judges are evaluating on-chain functionality, not CSS originality.

OPTION_C:
label: "Extend cto-web's Existing Design System"
description: Investigate the deployed `cto/cto-web` service, extract its design tokens and component patterns, and build the billing dashboard as a visual extension of the existing CTO product. Same typography, colors, spacing, component style.
visual_reference: Whatever cto-web currently looks like — the billing dashboard would be a new section/route within the same visual language.
strengths: Visual coherence with the existing CTO product. If cto-web already has React components, some can be reused directly. Judges who see both products recognize them as one ecosystem.
tradeoffs: Requires investigation time to audit cto-web's current stack and design patterns. If cto-web uses an older or different framework, adaptation costs may exceed building fresh. The billing dashboard has different information architecture needs (wallet-centric, on-chain data) that may not fit cto-web's existing patterns. For a hackathon, independence is faster than integration.

REASONING:
The `tweakcn` service deployed in the cluster is a strong signal that the team already uses shadcn/ui — tweakcn is specifically a shadcn/ui theme customization tool. This makes Option A the natural choice: the team has existing familiarity, the tooling is already deployed, and shadcn/ui's dark-mode defaults align perfectly with the Solana ecosystem aesthetic. For a hackathon where on-chain functionality is the judging criteria, spending extra days on custom CSS (Option B) or reverse-engineering an existing codebase (Option C) is a poor use of time. Ship fast with shadcn/ui, customize the theme via tweakcn, and spend the saved days on polishing the Anchor program and demo video.
```

---

## Summary of Recommendations

| Decision | Recommendation | Rationale (one line) |
|---|---|---|
| **dp-10** Wallet & Identity | **C: Dual Mode** | Resilient demo — works without extensions, but wallet-connect is there for the live flow |
| **dp-11** Dashboard Scope | **C: Hybrid (Read + Deposit/Withdraw)** | Highest demo impact per hour of dev time — money moving on-chain via UI |
| **dp-12** Component Library | **A: shadcn/ui + Tailwind 4** | Team already has tweakcn deployed; fastest path to a polished dark-mode Web3 dashboard |

### Design System Note

For the Stitch candidate (stitch-web-1): I recommend using it as **layout reference** for information hierarchy and page structure, then implementing with shadcn/ui components and a custom dark theme tuned via tweakcn. The Stitch output gives us the spatial logic; shadcn/ui gives us the component quality and accessibility guarantees.

### Verification Checklist

- [x] Every design decision point (dp-10, dp-11, dp-12) addressed
- [x] Each option has label, description, strengths, and tradeoffs
- [x] A recommendation is stated for every decision
- [x] Reasoning references the PRD, user needs, or project constraints
- [x] No option is a strawman (each is defensible)
- [x] Visual references include Stitch candidate where applicable
